Identification.LongTitle: An Act to establish the Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims to provide for the filing, negotiation and resolution of specific claims and to make related amendments to other Acts

Provision.Text: This enactment establishes the Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims including the Commission Division to help first nations and the Crown resolve specific claims and the Tribunal Division to decide certain issues arising from those claims if they cannot be otherwise resolved, and makes related amendments to other Acts.
Her Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows:

Section.Text: This Act may be cited as the Specific Claims Resolution Act.
The following definitions apply in this Act.

Definition.Text: Centre means the Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims established under section 4.
claim limit means the maximum under paragraph 56(1)(a).
claimant means a first nation whose specific claim has been filed.
Commission means the Commission Division of the Centre described in Part 2.
Crown means Her Majesty in right of Canada.

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a band as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Indian Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a group of persons that was, but is no longer, a band within the meaning of paragraph (a) and that has, under a land claims agreement, retained the right to bring a specific claim; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a group of persons that was a band within the meaning of paragraph (a), that is no longer a band by virtue of an Act or agreement mentioned in the schedule and that has not released its right to bring a specific claim.

Definition.Text: interlocutory issue means an issue in relation to which an application may be made to the Tribunal under section 47.
land claims agreement has the same meaning as in subsection 35(3) of the Constitution Act, 1982.

Minister means the Minister of Indian Affairs and Northern Development or such other member of the Queen’s Privy Council for Canada as may be designated by the Governor in Council as the Minister for the purposes of this Act.
party, in respect of a specific claim, means any claimant and the Crown, and any province added as a party under section 37 or 60.
specific claim means a claim that is filed under section 26.
Tribunal means the Tribunal Division of the Centre described in Part 3.

Section.Text: The purpose of this Act is to establish the Centre, including the Commission to help first nations and the Crown resolve specific claims and the Tribunal to decide certain issues arising from those claims.

Heading.Label: PART 1
Heading.TitleText: CANADIAN CENTRE FOR THE INDEPENDENT RESOLUTION OF FIRST NATIONS SPECIFIC CLAIMS

Section.Text: The Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims is hereby established.
The Centre consists of the Chief Executive Officer — to be appointed by the Governor in Council on the recommendation of the Minister — and the Commission and the Tribunal.
The Centre is responsible for

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: administering the affairs of the Commission and, with the exception of any matters assigned under this Act to the Tribunal or its Chief Adjudicator, those of the Tribunal;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: providing, at its expense, appropriate translation and interpretation services in connection with the resolution of specific claims under this Act; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: obtaining, developing and distributing educational materials for public information in respect of specific claims and fostering public understanding of this Act, including an understanding of the role and activities of the Commission and the Tribunal.

Section.Text: The Chief Executive Officer has supervision over and direction of the work and staff of the Centre.

Subsection.Text: The Chief Executive Officer shall hold office during good behaviour for a term of not more than five years and may be removed for cause by the Governor in Council.
The Chief Executive Officer is eligible for re-appointment on the expiration of any term of office.
The Chief Executive Officer shall be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council.

If the Chief Executive Officer is appointed full-time, he or she shall devote the whole of his or her time to the performance of the duties of the office and is entitled to be paid reasonable travel and living expenses incurred in the course of carrying out his or her duties under this Act while absent from his or her ordinary place of work, but the payment may not exceed the maximum limits for those expenses authorized by the Treasury Board for employees of the Government of Canada.

If the Chief Executive Officer is appointed part-time, he or she is entitled to be paid reasonable travel and living expenses incurred in the course of carrying out his or her duties under this Act while absent from his or her ordinary place of residence, but the payment may not exceed the maximum limits for those expenses authorized by the Treasury Board for employees of the Government of Canada.

The Chief Executive Officer shall not accept or hold any office or employment or carry on any activity inconsistent with the duties and functions of that office, but, for greater certainty, the Chief Executive Officer may also hold the office of Chief Commissioner of the Commission.

The Chief Executive Officer is deemed to be employed in the public service of Canada for the purposes of the Government Employees Compensation Act and any regulations made under section 9 of the Aeronautics Act.
The Chief Executive Officer has the rank and status of a deputy head for the purposes of the Financial Administration Act.

In the event of the absence or incapacity of the Chief Executive Officer, or if the office of Chief Executive Officer is vacant, the Chief Commissioner of the Commission — or if the offices of Chief Executive Officer and Chief Commissioner are held by the same person, the Vice-Chief Commissioner of the Commission — has the powers and duties of the Chief Executive Officer.

Section.Text: The Centre is a separate employer under the Public Service Staff Relations Act.

The Centre has exclusive authority in respect of personnel management and employer and employee relations, including the right to hire any staff it considers necessary for the proper conduct of the work of the Commission and of the Tribunal, to determine the terms and conditions of their employment and to terminate their employment, and may, in the exercise of that authority,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: determine its requirements with respect to human resources and provide for the allocation and effective utilization of human resources;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: implement an employment equity program;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: determine requirements for the training and development of its personnel and fix the terms and conditions on which that training and development may be carried out;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: provide for the classification of positions and employees;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: determine and regulate its employees’ pay, hours of work, leave and any related matters;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: provide for the awards that may be made to its employees for outstanding performance of their duties, for other meritorious achievement in relation to those duties and for inventions or practical suggestions for improvements;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: establish standards of discipline for its employees and fix the financial and other penalties, including termination of employment and suspension, that may be applied for breaches of discipline or misconduct and the circumstances and manner in which and the authority by which or by whom those penalties may be applied, or be varied or rescinded in whole or in part;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: provide for the termination of employment or the demotion to a position at a lower maximum rate of pay, for reasons other than breaches of discipline or misconduct, of its employees and establish the circumstances and manner in which and the authority by which or by whom those measures may be taken, or be varied or rescinded in whole or in part;

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: determine and regulate the payments that may be made to its employees by way of reimbursement for travel or other expenses and by way of allowances in respect of expenses and conditions arising out of their employment; and

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: provide for any other matters that the Centre considers necessary for effective personnel management.

Subsection.Text: The Centre may establish benefit programs, including group insurance programs, for its employees, may set any terms and conditions in respect of those programs, including those relating to contributions, premiums, expenditures to be made from those contributions and premiums, benefits and the management, control and audit of the programs, may make contributions and pay premiums in respect of those programs and may enter into contracts for those purposes.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Financial Administration Act does not apply
Subsection.Text: The Financial Administration Act does not apply to any contributions made or premiums paid by the Centre or the members of any program established under subsection (1) in respect of the program or any benefits received by those members.

For the purpose of deployments or appointments made, or closed competitions held, under the Public Service Employment Act, employees of the Centre shall be treated as if they were employees within the meaning of the Public Service Employment Act and had the rights of recourse provided by that Act.

Subsection.MarginalNote: When deployments made subject to terms
Subsection.Text: The Public Service Commission may, in consultation with the Treasury Board and the Centre, set terms and conditions for the deployment of Centre employees to departments and agencies under the Public Service Employment Act if, in its opinion, the principles governing the Centre’s staffing program are incompatible with those governing staffing under that Act.
When the Centre considers employees within the meaning of the Public Service Employment Act for employment within the Centre, it shall treat them as if they were employees of the Centre.

Section.Text: The Public Service Commission may periodically review the compatibility of the principles governing the Centre’s staffing program with those governing staffing under the Public Service Employment Act and, in that case, shall report its findings to the Centre.

Sections 32 to 34 of the Public Service Employment Act apply to the Chief Executive Officer, commissioners, adjudicators and employees of the Centre as if the Chief Executive Officer, commissioners and adjudicators were deputy heads, and the employees were employees, as defined in subsection 2(1) of that Act.
The offices of the Centre shall be in the National Capital Region as described in the schedule to the National Capital Act.
The accounts and financial transactions of the Centre shall be audited annually by the Auditor General of Canada, and a report of the audit shall be made to the Centre and to the Minister.

Subsection.Text: The Centre shall submit a report on the work of the Centre in a fiscal year to the Minister within six months after the end of that fiscal year, including the financial statements of the Centre and the report on them of the Auditor General of Canada.
The Minister shall cause a copy of the report to be laid before each House of Parliament on any of the first 30 days on which that House is sitting after the report is submitted to the Minister.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Available to first nations and the public
Subsection.Text: The Centre shall make the report available for public scrutiny at its offices and shall provide a copy of the report to a first nation on request.

Section.Text: Every three months, on the dates that the Minister specifies, the Centre shall report to the Minister

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the total amount of compensation payable in respect of specific claims resolved other than by a decision of the Tribunal during that quarter; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the total amount of compensation payable in respect of specific claims resolved by a decision of the Tribunal during that quarter.

Subsection.Text: The Commission Division of the Centre consists of a Chief Commissioner, a Vice-Chief Commissioner and up to five other commissioners to be appointed by the Governor in Council on the recommendation of the Minister.
The Governor in Council may, on the recommendation of the Minister, at any time increase or reduce the number of commissioners.
The Chief Commissioner and Vice-Chief Commissioner shall be appointed as full-time commissioners, and other commissioners may be appointed as full-time or part-time commissioners.
The Chief Commissioner and the Vice-Chief Commissioner shall hold office during good behaviour for a term of not more than five years and may be removed for cause by the Governor in Council.
The other commissioners shall hold office during good behaviour for a term of not more than three years and may be removed for cause by the Governor in Council.
A commissioner is eligible for re-appointment on the expiration of any term of office in the same or another capacity.

Full-time commissioners shall devote the whole of their time to the performance of the duties of their office and shall be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council. They are entitled to be paid reasonable travel and living expenses incurred by them in the course of carrying out their duties under this Act while absent from their ordinary place of work, but the payment may not exceed the maximum limits for those expenses authorized by the Treasury Board for employees of the Government of Canada.

Part-time commissioners shall be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council. They are entitled to be paid reasonable travel and living expenses incurred by them in the course of carrying out their duties under this Act while absent from their ordinary place of residence, but the payment may not exceed the maximum limits for those expenses authorized by the Treasury Board for employees of the Government of Canada.
Commissioners shall not accept or hold any office or employment or carry on any activity inconsistent with their duties and functions as commissioner.
Commissioners are deemed to be employed in the public service of Canada for the purposes of the Government Employees Compensation Act and any regulations made under section 9 of the Aeronautics Act.
The Chief Commissioner is responsible for the management of the business and affairs of the Commission.
In the event of the absence or incapacity of the Chief Commissioner, or if the office of Chief Commissioner is vacant, the Vice-Chief Commissioner has the powers and duties of the Chief Commissioner.

Section.Text: The Commission is responsible for

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: administering funding for the research, preparation and conduct by first nations of specific claims;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: assisting the parties in the effective use of appropriate dispute resolution processes at any time to facilitate the resolution of specific claims under this Act; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: referring to the Tribunal issues of validity or compensation.

Section.Text: The Commission, in carrying out its functions, may

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: make rules of procedure for specific claims under this Act, except with respect to proceedings before the Tribunal;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: establish, in accordance with any appropriation or allotment of funds to the Centre for these purposes, criteria for the provision of funding to first nations for research, preparation and conduct of specific claims, and allocate the funds in accordance with those criteria;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: arrange for any research, or expert or technical studies, agreed to by the parties;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: assist the parties to resolve any interlocutory issues; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: foster, at all times, the effective use of appropriate dispute resolution processes — including facilitated negotiation, mediation, non-binding arbitration and, with the consent of the parties, binding arbitration — for the resolution of specific claims.

Section.Text: No person who participates or has participated in the administration of funding for the research, preparation or conduct of a specific claim may, in respect of the claim, attend a preparatory meeting under section 28 or participate in any capacity in a dispute resolution process.

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsections (2) and (3), a first nation may file with the Commission a claim based on any of the following grounds, for compensation for its losses arising from those grounds:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: breach of — or failure to fulfil — a legal obligation of the Crown, including a fiduciary obligation,

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: that relates to the provision of lands or other assets and that arises from an agreement between the first nation and the Crown or from a treaty,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: under any legislation — pertaining to Indians or lands reserved for the Indians — of Canada or of a colony of Great Britain of which at least some portion now forms part of Canada, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: that arises out of the Crown’s administration of reserve lands, Indian moneys or other assets of the claimant;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: an illegal lease or disposition by the Crown of reserve lands;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: failure to provide compensation for reserve lands taken or damaged by the Crown or any of its agencies under legal authority; or

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: fraud by employees or agents of the Crown in connection with the acquisition, leasing or disposition of reserve lands.

Subsection.Text: A first nation may not file a claim that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: is based on events that occurred within the 15 years immediately preceding the filing of the claim;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: is based on a land claims agreement entered into after December 31, 1973, or any related agreement or Act of Parliament;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: is based on an Act of Parliament or agreement that is mentioned in the schedule, or an Act of Parliament or agreement for the implementation of such an Act or agreement;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: concerns the delivery or funding of programs or services related to policing, regulatory enforcement, corrections, education, health, child protection or social assistance, or of any similar public programs or services;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: is based on any agreement between the first nation and the Crown that provides for another mechanism for the resolution of disputes arising from the agreement; or

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: is based on, or alleges, aboriginal rights or title.

Subsection.Text: A first nation may not file a claim if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: there are proceedings before a court or tribunal other than the Tribunal that relate to the same assets and could result in a decision irreconcilable with that of the claim, or that are based on the same or substantially the same facts;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the first nation and the Crown are parties to those proceedings; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the proceedings have not been adjourned.

Subsection.Text: In the application of paragraph (1)(a) in respect of any legal obligation that was to be performed in an area within Canada’s present boundaries before that area became part of Canada, a reference to the Crown includes the Sovereign of Great Britain to the extent that the legal obligation or any liability relating to its breach or non-fulfilment became — or would, apart from any rule or doctrine that had the effect of limiting claims or prescribing rights against the Crown because of passage of time or delay, have become — the responsibility of the Crown in right of Canada.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Extended meaning of Crown — illegal lease or disposition
Subsection.Text: In the application of paragraph (1)(b) in respect of an illegal lease or disposition of reserve land located in an area within Canada’s present boundaries before that area became part of Canada, a reference to the Crown includes the Sovereign of Great Britain to the extent that liability for the illegal lease or disposition became — or would, apart from any rule or doctrine that had the effect of limiting claims or prescribing rights against the Crown because of passage of time or delay, have become — the responsibility of the Crown in right of Canada.

In the application of paragraphs (1)(c) and (d) in respect of reserve lands located in an area within Canada’s present boundaries, a reference to the Crown includes the Sovereign of Great Britain for the period before that area became part of Canada.

Section.Text: On the filing of a specific claim, the Commission shall provide a copy of it and its supporting documentation to the Minister.

Subsection.Text: The Commission shall, on the filing of a specific claim, convene a preparatory meeting of the parties for the purpose of identifying and clarifying the basis of the claim and the matters of fact or law on which the claimant relies in support of the claim, any additional research required and any other issue relevant to the preparation of the claim for consideration by the Minister.

The Commission, on the request of a party, may convene additional preparatory meetings and, when appropriate, community meetings in order to permit the involvement of other interested persons including elders, members of the claimant and relevant public officials.

Section.Text: At any time during or on the completion of the preparatory meetings, the claimant may make any amendments to its specific claim that it desires in order to fully and clearly set out the basis for its claim and the matters of fact or law on which it relies in support of the claim. The Commission shall provide a copy of any such amendments to the Minister.

Subsection.Text: On the completion of the preparatory meetings relating to a specific claim and receipt of any amendments under section 29, the Commission shall suspend proceedings in relation to the claim until it receives in writing the Minister’s decision on whether the Minister will negotiate the claim.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Delay not to be considered for validity
Subsection.Text: The Minister, in making a decision on whether to negotiate a claim, shall not consider any rule or doctrine that would have the effect of limiting claims or prescribing rights against the Crown because of passage of time or delay.

The Minister shall, at least every six months after the completion of the preparatory meetings, report to the Commission on the status of the review, the expected date of the Minister’s decision and, if applicable, the reasons why more time is required than previously expected.
No passage of time in relation to the decision on whether to negotiate a claim may be considered as constituting a decision not to negotiate the claim.

Section.Text: On receipt of the Minister’s decision not to negotiate a specific claim, the Commission shall, on the request of the claimant, assist the parties to attempt to resolve the issue of validity using any appropriate dispute resolution process.

Subsection.Text: On the request of the claimant, the Commission shall refer the issue of the validity of a specific claim to the Tribunal if it is satisfied that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the basis for the claim and all matters of fact and law on which the claimant relies in support of the claim have been fully and clearly identified and adequately researched and have been considered by the Minister;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: all dispute resolution processes appropriate for resolving the issue have been exhausted without the issue having been resolved; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the claimant has, in prescribed form, waived any compensation for the claim that is in excess of the claim limit as it applies to the claim in accordance with section 56.

Subsection.Text: When it refers the issue of validity to the Tribunal, the Commission shall provide to the Tribunal a copy of all documents that it has provided to the Minister under sections 27 and 29.

On the request of a party, the Commission shall, together with the issue of validity, refer to the Tribunal the issue of the extent, if any, of each respondent party’s responsibility with regard to each claimant for the claim.

Section.Text: After the Minister decides to negotiate a specific claim, or the Tribunal decides that a specific claim is valid, the Commission shall assist the parties to resolve the issue of compensation using any appropriate dispute resolution process.

At any time after the decision of the Minister or Tribunal referred to in section 33, the claimant may make any amendments to its specific claim that it desires in order to fully and clearly set out its position with respect to compensation and the matters of fact or law on which it relies in support of that position. The Commission shall provide a copy of any such amendments to the Minister.

Subsection.Text: On the request of the claimant, the Commission shall refer the issue of compensation for a specific claim to the Tribunal if it is satisfied that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the claimant’s position with respect to compensation and all matters of fact or law on which the claimant relies in support of that position have been fully and clearly identified and adequately researched and have been considered by the Minister in the course of a dispute resolution process under section 33;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: all dispute resolution processes appropriate for resolving the issue have been exhausted without the issue having been resolved;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: no compensation other than monetary compensation is being claimed;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the claimant has, in prescribed form, waived any compensation for the claim that is in excess of the claim limit as it applies to the claim in accordance with section 56; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the result of the following calculation is an amount not less than the claim limit:

FormulaDefinition.Text: is the maximum amount of compensation that may be awarded by the Tribunal in a fiscal year as published by the Minister from time to time in the Canada Gazette on the recommendation of the Minister of Indian Affairs and Northern Development and with the approval of the Treasury Board,
is the total amount of compensation awarded by the Tribunal in the current fiscal year,
is the number of specific claims in respect of which the issue of compensation has been referred to the Tribunal but has not been decided.

Subsection.Text: When it refers the issue of compensation to the Tribunal, the Commission shall provide to the Tribunal a copy of the documents that it has provided to the Minister under sections 27, 29 and 34.

On the request of a party, the Commission shall, together with the issue of compensation, refer to the Tribunal the issue of the extent, if any, of each respondent party’s responsibility with regard to each claimant for the claim.

The Commission shall give notice of the filing of a specific claim to each province, first nation or person whose interests a party indicates in writing to the Commission might be significantly affected by the claim.
Failure to provide notice does not invalidate any resolution of a specific claim under this Part.

Section.Text: On the request of the parties, the Commission shall allow a province, first nation or person to be consulted during a dispute resolution process under this Part, or a province or first nation to participate as a party.

Section.MarginalNote: No disclosure, production or testimony without consent — Chief Executive Officer, commissioner or employee

Subsection.Text: Subject to section 75, the Chief Executive Officer, a commissioner or an employee of the Centre who obtains any document or information related to a specific claim in the course of their appointment or employment, unless the parties to the claim consent, may not be compelled to, and shall not, disclose, produce or give evidence about the document or information.

Subsection.MarginalNote: No disclosure, production or testimony without consent — contractor
Subsection.Text: Subject to section 75, a person whose services are engaged by the Centre in respect of a specific claim and who obtains any document or information in the course of their engagement, unless the parties to the claim consent, may not be compelled to, and shall not, disclose, produce or give evidence about the document or information.

Section.MarginalNote: Prohibition on representing parties — Chief Executive Officer, commissioner or employee

Subsection.Text: The Chief Executive Officer, a commissioner or an employee of the Centre may not represent a party at a Tribunal hearing.

A person whose services are engaged by the Centre in respect of a specific claim may not represent a party at a Tribunal hearing in respect of the claim or any other claim based on the same or substantially the same facts.

Section.MarginalNote: Evidence not admissible in other proceedings
Section.Text: Evidence of anything said, any position taken or any admission made by any person in the course of a dispute resolution process under this Part is not admissible, without the consent of all parties, before the Tribunal or in any other proceeding.

Subsection.Text: The Tribunal Division of the Centre consists of a Chief Adjudicator, a Vice-Chief Adjudicator and up to five other adjudicators to be appointed by the Governor in Council on the recommendation of the Minister.

The majority of the adjudicators, including one of the Chief Adjudicator or the Vice-Chief Adjudicator, must be members in good standing of the bar of a province or the Chambre des notaires du Québec.
The Governor in Council may, on the recommendation of the Minister, at any time increase or reduce the number of adjudicators.
The Chief Adjudicator and Vice-Chief Adjudicator shall be appointed as full-time adjudicators, and other adjudicators may be appointed as full-time or part-time adjudicators.
The Chief Adjudicator and the Vice-Chief Adjudicator shall hold office during good behaviour for a term of not more than five years and may be removed for cause by the Governor in Council.
The other adjudicators shall hold office during good behaviour for a term of not more than three years and may be removed for cause by the Governor in Council.
An adjudicator is eligible for re-appointment on the expiration of any term of office in the same or another capacity.

Full-time adjudicators shall devote the whole of their time to the performance of the duties of their office and shall be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council. They are entitled to be paid reasonable travel and living expenses incurred by them in the course of carrying out their duties under this Act while absent from their ordinary place of work, but the payment may not exceed the maximum limits for those expenses authorized by the Treasury Board for employees of the Government of Canada.

Part-time adjudicators shall be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council. They are entitled to be paid reasonable travel and living expenses incurred by them in the course of carrying out their duties under this Act while absent from their ordinary place of residence, but the payment may not exceed the maximum limits for those expenses authorized by the Treasury Board for employees of the Government of Canada.
Adjudicators shall not accept or hold any office or employment or carry on any activity inconsistent with their duties and functions as adjudicator.
Adjudicators are deemed to be employed in the public service of Canada for the purposes of the Government Employees Compensation Act and any regulations made under section 9 of the Aeronautics Act.
The Chief Adjudicator is responsible for the management of the business and affairs of the Tribunal, including the following responsibilities:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: to strike panels to conduct hearings and decide issues before the Tribunal;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: to lead the Tribunal in the exercise of its power to make rules under subsection 45(1); and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: to provide guidance and assistance to panels.

Subsection.Text: In the event of the absence or incapacity of the Chief Adjudicator or if the office of Chief Adjudicator is vacant, the Vice-Chief Adjudicator has the powers and duties of the Chief Adjudicator.

Section.Text: The Tribunal is responsible to hold hearings into and decide issues before it relating to specific claims.

Subsection.Text: The Tribunal may make rules governing the proceedings, practice and procedures of panels, including rules governing

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: giving of notice;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: presentation of the positions of the parties with respect to issues before the Tribunal and of matters of fact or law on which the parties rely in support of their positions;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: summoning of witnesses;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: production and service of documents;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: discovery proceedings;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: taking and preservation of evidence before the start of a hearing;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: pre-hearing conferences;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: introduction of evidence;

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: imposition of time limits; and

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: costs, which rules shall accord with the rules of the Federal Court, with any modifications that may be required.

Subsection.Text: The Tribunal shall make its rules available to the public and, if possible, publish them in the First Nations Gazette or a similar publication.

Section.Text: A panel of the Tribunal may

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: determine any questions of law or fact in relation to any matter within its jurisdiction under this Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: order that specific claims be heard together or consecutively if they have issues of law or fact in common;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: order that specific claims be decided together if decisions of the claims could be irreconcilable or if they are subject to one claim limit under subsection 56(2);

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: delay or suspend a hearing or delay its decision of an issue

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: to await any court decision that the panel reasonably expects will assist it in its hearing or decision,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: to allow the parties to make further efforts to resolve an issue,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: to permit further preparations by a party, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: for any other reason that the panel considers appropriate;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: in the same manner and to the same extent as a superior court of record, summon and enforce the attendance of witnesses and compel them to give oral or written evidence on oath and to produce the documents and things that the panel considers necessary to a full hearing and consideration of the matter before it;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: receive and accept any evidence, including oral history, and other information, whether on oath or by affidavit or otherwise, that it sees fit, whether or not that evidence or information is or would be admissible in a court of law, unless it would be inadmissible in a court by reason of any privilege under the law of evidence;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: in applying the Tribunal’s rules of practice and procedure, take into consideration cultural diversity;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: lengthen or shorten any time limit established by the Tribunal’s rules of practice and procedure; and

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: award costs in accordance with the Tribunal’s rules of practice and procedure.

Section.Text: On application by a party to a specific claim, the Tribunal may, at any time, determine

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in relation to a specific claim that is before the Commission, to summon witnesses or to order production of documents;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: whether the claim and any other specific claim should be heard together or consecutively, or decided together; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: any other issue that needs to be resolved for a dispute resolution process in relation to the specific claim to proceed, if all other parties consent.

Section.Text: On an application to determine an interlocutory issue, the Chief Adjudicator shall strike a panel of one adjudicator who must be a member in good standing of the bar of a province or the Chambre des notaires du Québec, or of three adjudicators, at least one of whom must be such a member, to decide the issue. If a panel of three adjudicators has been struck, the Chief Adjudicator shall designate one of them to chair the panel, but the Chief Adjudicator shall chair the panel if he or she is a member of it.

A panel shall, after providing notice to the parties, hold a hearing, at the time and place that the panel considers most appropriate, into the interlocutory issue in respect of which the panel was struck, and make a decision on it.
On application by a party to a specific claim, the Tribunal may, at any time in whole or in part, order that the claim be struck out, with or without leave to amend, on the ground that it

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: is, on its face, not admissible under section 26;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: has not been filed by a first nation;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: is frivolous, vexatious or premature; or

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: may not, under section 74, be continued.

Section.Text: On application by a party to a specific claim, the Tribunal may, at any time, decide

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: whether the claim is, together with any other specific claim, subject to one claim limit under subsection 56(2); or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any other issue, if all other parties consent.

Section.Text: On an application under section 50 or 51, or the referral to the Tribunal of an issue of validity or compensation, the Chief Adjudicator shall strike a panel of three or five adjudicators, at least one of whom must be a member in good standing of the bar of a province or the Chambre des notaires du Québec. The Chief Adjudicator shall designate one of them to chair the panel, but the Chief Adjudicator shall chair the panel if he or she is a member of it.

A panel shall, after providing notice to the parties, hold a hearing, at the time and place that the panel considers most appropriate, into the issue in respect of which the panel was struck, and make a decision on it.

In deciding an issue of the validity of a specific claim, a panel shall not consider any rule or doctrine that would have the effect of limiting claims or prescribing rights against the Crown because of passage of time or delay.

For greater certainty, a panel, in making a decision on the extent of responsibility of each respondent party to a specific claim, may determine that any respondent is, or all the respondents together are, not responsible or not wholly responsible for the claim.

Section.MarginalNote: Basis and limitations for decision on compensation

Subsection.Text: A panel, in making a decision on an issue of compensation for a specific claim,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: shall calculate the pecuniary losses in relation to the claim, to the prescribed maximum, or, if none is prescribed, to a maximum of ten million dollars, based on principles of compensation applied by courts;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: may not include any amount for

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: any harm or loss that is not pecuniary in nature; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: shall award compensation against each respondent party proportional to the party’s responsibility for the losses calculated under paragraph (a).

Subsection.MarginalNote: One claim limit for related claims
Subsection.Text: Two or more specific claims shall, for the purposes of the application of the claim limit under paragraph (1)(a), be treated as one claim if they

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: are made by the same claimant and are based on the same or substantially the same facts; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: are made by different claimants, are based on the same or substantially the same facts and relate to the same assets.

Subsection.Text: If claims are treated as one claim under paragraph (2)(b), the panel shall apportion equitably among the claimants the total amount awarded.

If compensation is awarded under this Act for an unlawful disposition of all of the interests or rights of a claimant in or to land and the interests or rights have never been restored to the claimant, then, despite section 39 of the Indian Act, all the claimant’s interests in, and rights to, the land are extinguished, without prejudice to any right of the claimant to bring any proceeding related to that unlawful disposition against a province that is not a party to the specific claim.

Despite section 39 of the Indian Act, if compensation is awarded under this Act in relation to the unexpired period of a lease of land entered into by the Crown in contravention of the rights of the claimant, then, for the duration of that period, the persons who, if the lease had been lawful, would have had any interest in, or right to enjoy, the land are deemed to have that interest or right.

A person who has ceased to be an adjudicator of the Tribunal may, with the authorization of the Chief Adjudicator and for the period that the Chief Adjudicator fixes, take part in the disposition of any matter in which the person became engaged while holding office as an adjudicator, and a person so authorized is, for that purpose, deemed to be an adjudicator.

If an adjudicator who participates in a panel charged with deciding an issue is, for any reason, unable to take part in the decision, the Chief Adjudicator may, taking into account sections 48 and 52, authorize the remaining adjudicators of the panel to make the decision.

If a panel’s decision of an issue in relation to a specific claim might, in the opinion of the panel, significantly affect the interests of a province, first nation or person that has not previously been notified of the claim under subsection 36(1), the Tribunal shall so notify them. The parties may make submissions to the panel as to whose interests might be affected.
Failure to provide notice does not invalidate any decision of the panel.

Section.Text: The Tribunal shall grant status as a party in a specific claim to a province that has agreed to submit to the jurisdiction of the Tribunal for that claim.

Subsection.Text: Subject to this Act, a panel shall conduct its hearings in any manner that it considers fit.

In deciding how to conduct a hearing, a panel shall have regard to any submissions that a party has made regarding the manner in which the hearing is to be conducted and to the importance of achieving an expeditious resolution.
Subject to subsection (2), panel hearings shall be public.

A panel may, on application by a party, take any appropriate measures and make any order that the panel considers necessary to ensure the confidentiality of a hearing if the panel is satisfied that the reasons for confidentiality outweigh the societal interest that the hearing be conducted in public.

Section.Text: A party may cross-examine a witness

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: as of right, if the witness is called by a party adverse in interest; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: with leave of the panel, in any other case.

Section.Text: Subject to section 54, section 24 of the Crown Liability and Proceedings Act applies in respect of a specific claim as if it were a proceeding.

If a panel determines that specific claims are subject to one claim limit under subsection 56(2) or that they present common issues of law or fact that create a risk that decisions on the claims will be irreconcilable, it shall, unless the parties otherwise agree, decide the claims together.

If a panel, in considering a specific claim, determines that a first nation has a specific claim or a potential specific claim based on the same or substantially the same facts and relating to the same assets as the specific claim, or that there is any other specific claim or potential specific claim by a first nation that must be before the Tribunal to enable a full resolution of the specific claim, it shall suspend proceedings with respect to the specific claim until, in the case of another specific claim, it is before the Tribunal or, in the case of a potential specific claim, it has been filed under this Act and is before the Tribunal.

Subsection.Text: A party may withdraw an issue from the Tribunal at any time before the Tribunal gives its decision on it and, in such a case, the panel shall not render a decision on it.
A panel may award costs on the withdrawal of an issue from the Tribunal.
Withdrawal of an issue does not bar any subsequent consideration of the issue by the Tribunal.

Section.MarginalNote: Evidence not admissible in other proceedings
Section.Text: Subject to subsection 71(1), evidence of anything said, any position taken or any admission made by a person in the course of a panel hearing is not admissible in any other proceeding.

Section.MarginalNote: Advance notice of decision on compensation
Section.Text: Not later than 14 days before a panel renders its decision on an issue of compensation, the Tribunal shall give notice to the parties and to the Chief Executive Officer that the decision will be rendered.
A panel shall give written reasons for its decisions. The Tribunal shall cause the reasons and the decisions to be published in the manner that the Tribunal decides.

Subsection.Text: A decision of a panel is subject to judicial review under the Federal Court Act.

Subject to subsection (1), a panel’s decisions are not subject to appeal or review and, except for decisions with respect to interlocutory issues, are final and conclusive between the parties in all proceedings in any court or tribunal arising out of the same or substantially the same facts.

Despite subsection (2), a decision of a panel that a specific claim is valid is conclusive only under this Act and, except for the purposes of judicial review under subsection (1), any evidence of the decision is inadmissible before any other court or tribunal.

Section.Text: If a panel makes an order that a specific claim is invalid or awards compensation for a specific claim,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: each respondent party is released from any cause of action, claim or liability to the claimant and any of its members of any kind, direct or indirect, arising out of the same or substantially the same facts on which the claim is based; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the claimant shall indemnify each respondent party against any amount that the respondent party becomes liable to pay as a result of a claim, action or other proceeding for damages brought by the claimant or any of its members against any other person arising out of the same or substantially the same facts.

Subsection.Text: An award of compensation against the Crown may be paid by instalments, but the award must be fully paid within five years after the date of the Tribunal’s decision.

The unpaid balance of the award bears simple interest from the date of the award, at a rate equal to the lowest rate of interest quoted by banks to the most credit-worthy borrowers for prime business loans, as determined and published by the Bank of Canada for the month in which the award was made, which shall be paid together with each instalment.

Section.Text: A specific claim may not be continued if the claimant

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: commences, before another tribunal or a court, a proceeding against the Crown that is based on the same or substantially the same facts as the claim, or that relates to the same assets as the claim and could result in a decision irreconcilable with that of the claim, unless the claimant immediately has the proceeding adjourned; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: takes a new step in, or does not continue to adjourn, a proceeding mentioned in paragraph (a) or in subsection 26(3).

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsection (2), documents filed with the Commission or with the Tribunal are public documents.

The Commission, on the request of a party, or a panel of the Tribunal, on the application of a party, may take any measures that it considers necessary to ensure the confidentiality of a document if it is satisfied that the interest of a party or a person that the document not be disclosed outweighs the societal interest that it be public.

Not earlier than three years and not later than five years after the coming into force of this section, the Minister shall undertake and complete a review of the mandate and structure of the Centre, of its efficiency and effectiveness of operation and of any other matters related to this Act that the Minister considers appropriate. In carrying out the review, the Minister shall give to first nations an opportunity to make representations.

On completion of the review, the Minister shall cause to be prepared and sign a report that sets out a statement of any changes to this Act, including any changes to the functions, powers or duties of the Centre or either of its divisions, that the Minister recommends.

The Minister shall submit to each House of Parliament a copy of the report on any of the first 90 days on which that House is sitting after the Minister signs the report, and each House shall refer the report to the appropriate committee of that House.

Section.MarginalNote: Opportunity to make representations regarding appointments
Section.Text: The Minister shall, before making a recommendation under section 5 or subsection 20(1) or 41(1), notify claimants — which notification may be by ordinary mail sent to their latest known addresses — that they may, during a period that the Minister specifies of not less than 30 days after the date of the notice, make representations in respect of appointments to the office or offices in question.

Subsection.Text: At no time shall a person who was appointed under section 5 or subsection 20(1) or 41(1) act for any party in connection with any specific claim in relation to which they performed any work or concerning which they obtained significant information during their term in office.

Persons who were appointed under section 5 or subsection 20(1) or 41(1) shall not, within a period of one year after the end of their term in office, accept any employment with or enter into a contract for services with the Department of Indian Affairs and Northern Development or a first nation that had a pending specific claim — before the Commission or the Tribunal, in the case of the Chief Executive Officer, or, in the case of a commissioner or adjudicator, before the Division of the Centre to which the person was appointed — at any time during their term in office.

Section.Text: The Governor in Council may make regulations

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: adding to Part 2 of the schedule the name of any agreement related to aboriginal self-government; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: prescribing anything that may, under this Act, be prescribed.

Heading.Label: PART 5
Heading.TitleText: TRANSITIONAL PROVISION, CONSEQUENTIAL AMENDMENTS, COORDINATING AMENDMENT AND COMING INTO FORCE

Section.Text: During the period of one year after the coming into force of section 76.1, the reference in that section to “claimants” shall be read as a reference to “claimants under this Act or under the Specific Claims Policy of the Government of Canada”.
Schedule I to the Access to Information Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order under the heading “Other Government Institutions”:

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Centre canadien du règlement indépendant des revendications particulières des premières nations

Section.Text: Schedule II to the Act is amended by adding, in alphabetical order, a reference to

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Loi sur le règlement des revendications particulières

ContinuedSectionSubsection.Text: and a corresponding reference to “section 38 and subsections 62(2) and 75(2)”.

Section.Text: Schedule I.1 to the Financial Administration Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order in column I:

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Centre canadien du règlement indépendant des revendications particulières des premières nations

ContinuedSectionSubsection.Text: and a corresponding reference in column II to the “Minister of Indian Affairs and Northern Development”.

Section.Text: The schedule to the Privacy Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order under the heading “Other Government Institutions”:

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Centre canadien du règlement indépendant des revendications particulières des premières nations

Section.Text: Part II of Schedule I to the Public Service Staff Relations Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order:

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Centre canadien du règlement indépendant des revendications particulières des premières nations

Section.Text: Part I of Schedule I to the Public Service Superannuation Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order:

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Canadian Centre for the Independent Resolution of First Nations Specific Claims
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Centre canadien du règlement indépendant des revendications particulières des premières nations

Section.Text: On the later of the coming into force of section 14 of the Courts Administration Service Act, chapter 8 of the Statutes of Canada, 2002, and subsection 71(1) of this Act, subsection 71(1) of this Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A decision of a panel is subject to judicial review under the Federal Courts Act.

Section.Text: The provisions of this Act come into force on a day or days to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Loi sur les Cris et les Naskapis du Québec
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Kanesatake Interim Land Base Governance Act
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Loi sur le gouvernement du territoire provisoire de Kanesatake
Loi sur l’éducation des Mi’kmaq
Loi sur l’Accord définitif nisga’a
Loi sur l’autonomie gouvernementale de la bande indienne sechelte
Loi sur l’autonomie gouvernementale des premières nations du Yukon

BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Self-Government Agreement
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale des Premières Nations de Champagne et de Aishihik
Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale de la première nation de Little Salmon/Carmacks
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Nacho Nyak Dun First Nation Self-Government Agreement
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale de la première nation des Nacho Nyak Dun
Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale de la première nation de Selkirk
Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale du Conseil des Ta’an Kwach’an
Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale du conseil des Tlingits de Teslin
Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale des Tr’ondèk Hwëch’in
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemEn: Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation Self-Government Agreement
BilingualGroup.BilingualItemFr: Entente sur l’autonomie gouvernementale de la première nation des Gwitchin Vuntut
